title: The Obsession With Teenage Protagonists
wikitext:
[]: The Obsession With Teenage Protagonists             Quote Post by bogey » Sat Jan 05, 2013 6:21 pm I recognize this is not only an anime thing but with all media everywhere, but what do you make of the obsession with teenage protagonists? in anime though I feel its even more amplified. you see the exaltation of teenage characters in mecha shows especially where some 14-year old kids are the only ones capable of piloting giant robots to save the world. has a shounen ever even had an adult protagonist? why aren't there more adult protagonists in anime?

[]:             Quote Post by Redtophat » Sat Jan 05, 2013 6:24 pm Theres a split, it's mostly because of the demographic of the audience that the anime studio(s) want to appeal to with its shows. Also because mangaka/anime creators tend to like youthful characters more as they're usually more popular, but that's all a gross generalization.

[]:             Quote Post by Tankred » Sat Jan 05, 2013 6:32 pm lolJapan

[]:             Quote Post by riffraff11235 » Sat Jan 05, 2013 6:38 pm Teenagers are both old enough to interact meaningfully with the "adult world" and young enough to be heavily influenced by new ideas. Their personalities can easily be molded by the major events of the story.

[]:             Quote Post by moonwolf2024 » Sun Jan 06, 2013 1:45 am I thought i was only one who asked that question. I also wondered what the motive was behind this. I think the last mecha or semi mecha show i saw that didnt include some middle schooler learning to fight in a giant robot was Martian Successor Nadesco and Marcros Plus (I'm sure there's a few more i can't think of right now). And for some reason it's even more so now. If you watch older anime the characters were at least around 17, which would be a bit more undestandable than a 14 year old. Could they be trying to appeal to a particular target market......totally possible. Maybe they feel the story would be seen better from the perspective of a struggling young teen......another possibilty. Now it could also be a cultural thing. In Japan going to middle school is almost the equivalent to Americans going to High School. There was time when most of our prime time shows centered around High School students......could be a little of this as well But maybe I'm just giving them too much credit. As we all know....Japan is prone to some pretty weird and perverted shit....(I think it's whole lot of this)

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Jan 06, 2013 3:44 am Ayato Kamina was 17, and IIRC the Gundam 00 pilots were similar age. But even way back, the 19 year old Lovely Angels got referred to as old ladies on occasion -- it's not like the obsession with youth, and being chuuni is a new thing for this decade's anime; more a continuing "best days of your life" thing, before harsh reality closes in.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Sun Jan 06, 2013 11:51 am Breast humor, my friend; breast humor.

[]:             Quote Post by Squigsquasher » Sun Feb 10, 2013 4:53 pm I think it is largely down to the Japanese view that the teenage years are the heydey of your life before things like work and marriage come along and ruin everything. Evangelion partially deconstructed the trope by pointing out that these teenaged mecha pilots are essentially child soldiers, with all the mental imbalance that comes with it.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Feb 12, 2013 2:30 pm Well, it's just a good drama source: Young enough to be emo about your parents, old enough to be emo about your love life. Also, a school setting simply procvides a justification for very different people to be in the same room/interact with each other, people that would have very differemt jobs/ways of life if they were adults. There's not a big chance that you would see two people as different as, say, Shinji and Asuka anywhere near the same room if they were 25. And there's the fact that a young person is still "malleable" in their opinions and knowledge of the world - a teenager will already have something of an established identity/more capacity for critical reflection than, say, an elementary school kid, but be in a period of discovery and reconsideration, which is simply practical when you want to tell a story about someone finding out a certain philosophical truth/message, you can tell the story of them comming to that conclusion as they become adult, as you will generally think that your message/philosophic stance is the "adult" one - most people think their opinions are right. XD

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Tue Feb 12, 2013 7:11 pm Modern Japanese culture glorifies youth all around and for them the time of good times when they live for themselves ends with end of university and assimilation into "society" and company. The "adult life" is the least interesting and valuable part of Japanese life in society that places emphasis on the beginning and end of lives. As adults they're first of all workers for company as well as mothers and fathers. They're defined by duties their roles set first of all. Your average salaryman won't have time to go for epic adventures nor would it be approved by reading audience. This is the biggest reason why High School age, especially since HS era is so romanticized in Japanese tihnking in general, is the ideal for heroes and heroines of Japanese anime, manga, games etc. Other big reason, of course, is that as mostly youth oriented medium (well, manga has universal appeal and universal audiences though obv. really big titles target youth as a rule) the audience gets heroes close to them. When it comes to games while Japanese might be the most gamer nation in the world (I'm not entirely sure of this though) games are perceived - unlike in west today - as fare for children and youth and something distinct from adult realities. This explains certain differences between western and Japanese games. Talk of "adult market" for games in Japan is absurd unless you're talking of eroges. All these factors - and some others - make sure Japanese popular fiction revolves endlessly around teenagers and early years of adulthood. In short, unless you want to change modern Japanese culture in very large scale you won't see end of high school dominance in anime.

[]:             Quote Post by user-02 » Wed Feb 13, 2013 8:38 pm The teenage years are a very emotionally turbulent and resonant time in everyone's life, and they have always served as go-to literary and pop cultural reference points for broader commentary on life, society, and the human condition. Vaguely apropos of this discussion, there was a very interesting article in a magazine which escapes me at the moment (I want to say the New Yorker?) about precisely this topic. The author discussed the science of the teenage mind, and why the teenage years stay with us so profoundly throughout life. Apparently, our brains are at their most emotionally receptive in the teenage years: the highs and lows are amplified, whereas later in life, we (mostly) attain a more even keel. Because we literally feel the teenage years more acutely than we feel any others, they remain highly profound points of reference. Entertainment and literature have always drawn from this well, because it is so relatable: both to actual teenagers, and to the adults who very clearly remember being teenagers. Beyond that, sure, there is probably an idiosyncratic element of the Japanese culture that focuses especially on the teenage / high school experience. But I'd argue that American culture places an almost equally big emphasis on those years.

[]:             Quote Post by bogey » Tue Jun 04, 2013 2:50 am some good answers in here. do you think teenage years are just as romanticized in the west?

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Tue Jun 04, 2013 4:00 am bogey wrote: do you think teenage years are just as romanticized in the west?

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Tue Jun 04, 2013 4:28 am CJD wrote: Yes, but in a different way. Saved by the Bell being the first example that comes to mind. Of course you can't forget Wonder Years or Happy Days either, though Happy Days was more about romanticizing the 50's. I'd almost say we, as a species, are enamored with adolescence.

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Tue Jun 04, 2013 2:35 pm Teenage protagonists because audience can relate and then goes out and buys merchandise.

[]:             Quote Post by moonwolf2024 » Tue Jun 04, 2013 10:26 pm bogey wrote: some good answers in here. do you think teenage years are just as romanticized in the west?

[]:             Quote Post by Lorkhan » Tue Jun 04, 2013 11:06 pm Moral and emotional malleability mostly. It makes for more chances for characters to grow and devlope , and the inner workings of the setting can be explained to both them and the audience. As others said, they're (marginally, depending on the setting) capable of interacting and participating in the events that are going on. Older folks are generally a little more worldly then most people, and otherwise wouldn't question whats going on some of the time. Its also why such protagonists can hail from the country side, as they're generally removed from worldly affairs and are somewhat ignorant of whats what.

[]:             Quote Post by kuribo-04 » Wed Jun 05, 2013 9:55 am According to an interview that was just translated in the Everything Else trhread, Anno still felt like a fourteen-year old when he did Eva. "Shinji-kun is the current me."

[]:             Quote Post by Koneko_Hime » Wed Jun 05, 2013 3:31 pm There's the whole demographic thing, and like someone just said the drama of it all. there's just something about putting kids into war and them going through it. I think there's also kind of growing up a lot and that life is tough. I'm not really sure, I never thought of it, I only reccently got into mecha. to put it "War is hell"
